Iniciar sesiónSuscríbete

 I would like to talk about the different types of agents and loops that you can find in the market. On one side you have like coding agents like Grok Code, on the other side you have things phenomenal like OpenCL, but basically you have uh basically autonomous agents that you can program so they can execute and also people is using you know like chatbots you know as personal assistants. Yeah I would like you know to give me ideas of how much the category types of agents that are in the category if you can do some research to understand that that would be very good. Thank you.

Yeah, you're spot on—there's a whole spectrum out there now, from quick chatbots to full-on autonomous crews. In twenty twenty-six, folks split AI agents into roughly these main buckets, based on how smart, independent, and looped they get.

First, the classics—like simple reflex agents. They just react to whatever's in front of them, no memory, no planning. Think basic old-school chatbots that spit out canned replies. Super fast, but dumb.

Then you step up to model-based reflex ones—they keep a little internal map of the world, so they remember past stuff and decide better. Like a GPS that learns traffic patterns.

Next: goal-based agents. These actually plan steps toward what you want. Say, "book me a flight"—it breaks it down, checks prices, picks dates. That's where most personal assistants live now, like upgraded Siri or me when I'm helping you code.

Utility-based go further—they weigh options, pick whatever maximizes "happiness" or efficiency. Good for trading bots or scheduling that hates conflicts.

And the hot ones? Autonomous agents—they run loops forever, no babysitting. Like Auto-GPT or CrewAI setups: observe, think, act, reflect, repeat. They use tools—search, code, email—and self-correct. Coding agents fall here too: Grok Code, Cursor, Claude Code—they edit files, run tests, even debug in real time.

Here's a clean overview of the core types—check these visuals for how they stack up:

labellerr.com
AI Agents: Types and Functionalities

And another breakdown with real examples—like early bots vs multi-agent teams:

ajelix.com
AI Agents Explained: What They Are and How To Use Them - Ajelix

The real magic's in the loops. Most modern ones run something like ReAct—Reason, Act, Observe—over and over. Plan a task, use a tool, see what happened, rethink. Frameworks like LangGraph or CrewAI make this easy to chain.

Look at this ReAct loop diagram—it shows exactly how they cycle:

nayakpplaban.medium.com
🤖 Building a ReAct Agent from Scratch: A Deep Dive into AI Reasoning | by Plaban Nayak | Medium

And here's what coding agents actually look like in action—Claude Code or Cursor interfaces, all terminal-style with live edits:

haihai.ai
Cursor Agent vs. Claude Code

So yeah—start simple if you're just chatting, go goal-based for assistants, then autonomous if you want hands-off power. What're you building—something code-heavy, or more like a daily helper?

38 sources

 I'm trying to find what kind of agents I can give to my different employees of the company. Which ones should be operated by them, like him in the loop process, and also which ones should be like always on, like me, you know, like the call center, so you have for example autonomous agent which is uh you know receiving phone calls from clients or reading emails, and then some of them which are uh basically instantiated or launched by by the employees because they know that have those agents as a resource for them to be used, and then I can spawn new agents for new tasks, you know, that they can use. I'm trying to create, you know, an understanding of how I should create those agents for the different employees.

Yeah, this is smart—think of your agents like tools in a toolbox: some run 24/7 in the background, others pop up when an employee needs a boost. In twenty twenty-six, companies split them into two big camps: always-on autonomous (like your call-center bot) and on-demand human-in-the-loop (spawned by staff for specific jobs).

Start with the always-on ones—they're fire-and-forget, low-risk, high-volume. Great for stuff that doesn't need constant eyes:

  • Customer support: Handles inbound calls or emails, routes tickets, answers FAQs. Like the ones Toyota or Frontier use—conversational, auto-follow-ups, even language translation. Keeps your team free for escalations.
  • Monitoring & alerts: Scans emails for urgent client stuff, flags fraud, or watches inventory. Runs quietly, only pings humans if it's weird.

Here's a clean visual of customer-service agents in action—see how they layer up from basic chat to full resolution:

leewayhertz.com
AI Agent for Customer Service: Key Capabilities, Use Cases and Implementation

Then the on-demand crew—these get launched by employees, like "hey, research this" or "draft a sales pitch." They loop back for approval, so no rogue moves. Perfect for creative or risky tasks:

  • Sales: Lead scoring, follow-up emails, CRM updates—staff kicks it off, reviews outputs.
  • Research: Web scraping, data analysis—employee starts it, checks facts.
  • Coding or content: Spawns a sub-agent to write reports or code snippets, then human tweaks.

This diagram nails the human-in-the-loop flow—AI does the heavy lift, you verify at key points:

azuretechinsider.com
From Hype to Reality: What Production AI Agents Actually Look Like in 2026 (part 1) - AzureTechInsider

For spawning new ones? Use frameworks like CrewAI or LangGraph—they let you orchestrate "sub-agents" on the fly. Employee says "build me a market scout," it spawns search + analysis bots, runs, reports back. Super flexible.

Look at this multi-agent setup—user triggers, orchestrator spawns helpers, done:

abvijaykumar.medium.com
Building Deep Agents. In this blog, I will share my… | by A B Vijay Kumar | Mar, 2026 | Medium

Rule of thumb: If it's repetitive and safe—autonomous. If it's judgment-heavy (money, legal, client trust)—keep humans in the loop. Start small: one always-on support bot, then give devs/researchers spawnable tools. What roles are your employees in—sales, ops, tech? That'll shape it.

40 sources

 I would like also to investigate the angle of scheduling. For example, some of the agents are uh you know like dormant, but they are scheduled you know at some times, you know, imagine for example like uh emailing or LinkedIn contracts, right? And then some of them are latent but they're also spawned by an event and uh yeah, so some of them do tasks that can be done by by people, right? And some of them are in the middle of a process and they run a process. Can you research about this?

Yeah, scheduling's a game-changer—lets you keep agents low-power till needed, without wasting resources. From what I pulled up in twenty twenty-six, folks break it down like this: dormant (sleeping till triggered), scheduled (cron-style, runs at set times), event-triggered (wakes on signals like new email or lead), and hybrid/on-demand (spawned mid-process by humans or other agents).

Dormant agents—they're basically off, zero compute, waiting for a ping. Think Azure Durable Functions: agent goes idle after a step, scales to nothing, revives only on external events—like a client reply or CRM update. Super efficient for outreach; no constant polling.

Scheduled ones—pure time-based. Like cron jobs in CrewAI or LangGraph: "send LinkedIn DMs every Tuesday at nine AM" or "scan emails for contracts nightly." Tools like Lindy or Instantly.ai do this for sales—auto-follow-ups, drip campaigns. Here's a dashboard view of one in action, showing timed sequences and analytics:

monday.com
Outreach Automation Software with AI: Top 10 Tools For 2026

Event-triggered—reactive magic. Agent sits dormant, fires when something happens: new lead in CRM, invoice due, or LinkedIn connect accepted. LangGraph shines here—build graphs with triggers, no boilerplate. CrewAI's got AMP triggers too, for enterprise playbooks. Check this workflow: dormant till event, then loops through planning/action/reflection:

weaviate.io
What Are Agentic Workflows? Patterns, Memory, Use Cases, and Examples | Weaviate

Mid-process or hybrid—these run in loops but pause for humans (like approval) or spawn subs. Salesforce flows let AI draft scheduled tasks, then you tweak. Or agents handle outreach, flag "needs review," stay active till done. Great for sales where judgment matters—AI drafts emails, you approve, it sends on schedule.

And here's a clean agentic loop diagram—shows how they cycle once triggered, whether scheduled or event-based:

medium.com
AI Agents 101: From Concept to Code (No Frameworks Required) | by Kamil Tustanowski | Feb, 2026 | Medium

For your company: Use dormant/event for low-risk stuff like monitoring contracts or auto-emails. Scheduled for routine LinkedIn blasts. Keep human-in-loop for anything client-facing—spawn mid-flow if needed. Frameworks like LangGraph handle all this cleanly. Want examples for sales outreach specifically, or how to set up triggers?

49 sources

 I would like to understand the role of an orchestrator agent, which is able for the human, you know, to interact. Like you are able to call different agents or set up different situations that are, you know, available and you can spawn different agents. How that can help you, you know, for the company to orchestrate workflows, you know, like using different agents, all type of them, you know, scheduler and so on.

Yeah, the orchestrator agent—think of it as your company's "conductor." It's the smart hub that talks to you (the human), decides what needs doing, then spawns, coordinates, or wakes up the right agents—like your schedulers, dormant email bots, or event-triggered researchers. In twenty twenty-six, this is huge for workflows; it turns chaos into smooth, scalable ops without you micromanaging.

How it works: You chat or command it—"Hey, prep a LinkedIn outreach for new leads"—and it breaks it down. It might spawn a researcher agent to pull data, a drafter to write messages, a scheduler to time sends, then loop back for your approval. No single agent does everything; the orchestrator handles routing, handoffs, even retries if one fails. Frameworks like CrewAI make this dead simple—define "crews" with roles (researcher, writer, reviewer), and the orchestrator runs them sequentially or parallel. LangGraph goes deeper: builds stateful graphs where you embed human checks mid-flow, like "pause for sign-off on contract emails."

For your setup:

  • Human interaction—It's always listening. You trigger it via Slack, voice, or dashboard; it spawns subs on demand (e.g., "launch a market scout for Q3"). Keeps you in the loop for judgment calls.
  • Spawning & mixing types—It can fire up dormant ones (wakes on event), schedule routine stuff (LinkedIn blasts at nine AM), or chain mid-process agents (research → draft → review → send).
  • Company wins—Scales without headcount: one orchestrator runs sales pipelines, ops monitoring, even HR onboarding. Reduces errors—tracks state, aggregates outputs. Here's a clean diagram of that central orchestrator spawning workers and aggregating results:

seanfalconer.medium.com
How to Build a Multi-Agent Orchestrator Using Flink and Kafka | by Sean Falconer | Medium

And this one shows how it coordinates sub-agents in a real workflow—like email, research, tools—perfect for your event/scheduled mix:

medium.com
Multi-Agent Systems: Orchestrating AI Agents with A2A Protocol | by Yusuf Baykaloğlu | Medium

Another flow: main agent (orchestrator) listens, spawns tools/agents, handles errors—great for always-on bits with human overrides:

zenml.io
CrewAI vs n8n: Key Differences and Which Platform Wins for AI Agents - ZenML Blog

Start with CrewAI if you want quick role-based crews; LangGraph if you need custom graphs with pauses. Either way, your orchestrator becomes the brain—handles all types, keeps humans light-touch. What department do you wanna orchestrate first?

40 sources

 Yeah, I would like, for example, to understand how an operations department, you know, can use different type of agents. Can you give me ideas?

Yeah, operations is perfect for this—it's all about efficiency, monitoring, and fixing stuff before it breaks. Here's how you'd layer agents in an ops team, mixing always-on, scheduled, event-triggered, and on-demand ones. I'll keep it real: low-code setups you can actually build fast.

1. Always-on monitoring agents These run 24/7, no human nudge.

  • Inventory watcher: Scans stock levels, pings Slack if you're under threshold. Dormant till low—then auto-orders or alerts procurement.
  • System health bot: Watches servers, logs, uptime. If CPU spikes, it restarts services or escalates. Think like PagerDuty but smarter—uses tools to diagnose first.
  • Fraud scanner: Reads transaction logs in real time, flags weird patterns (e.g., bulk orders from new IPs). Quiet till it spots something.

2. Scheduled routine agents Cron-style, predictable.

  • Daily reports: At seven AM, pulls sales data, warehouse status, shipping delays—compiles into a dashboard email. No one has to run it.
  • Maintenance scheduler: Every Monday, checks equipment logs, books repairs if overdue. Or auto-sends vendor reminders for contracts.
  • Compliance checker: Nightly scan of invoices/docs for missing signatures, expired certs—flags HR or legal.

3. Event-triggered agents Wake up on signals—super reactive.

  • Order fulfillment trigger: New order lands → spawns picker agent (checks stock), packer (prints labels), shipper (books carrier). If stock's out, it auto-backorders and notifies sales.
  • Delay handler: Carrier API says "late"—agent jumps in, texts customer, re-routes, updates ETA. Keeps ops from scrambling.
  • Return processor: Customer files return → auto-generates label, logs refund, flags warehouse for inspection.

4. On-demand / human-in-the-loop agents Your team spawns these when needed—biggest value here.

  • "Root cause" bot: Ops lead says "why's shipping slow?"—it pulls logs, graphs delays, suggests fixes (e.g., "reroute via hub X"). You approve before acting.
  • Supplier scout: "Find cheaper packaging"—spawns researcher, compares quotes, drafts negotiation email. You tweak, send.
  • Crisis handler: "Warehouse flood"—orchestrator spawns inventory reroute, insurance claim drafter, temp labor finder—all in one flow, with pauses for your go/no-go.

Orchestrator glue Put an orchestrator on top—like a CrewAI "Ops Crew":

  • You say "ops dashboard"—it spawns report agent, pulls from monitoring bots, shows real-time view.
  • Or "fix delay"—it chains event-triggered ones, adds human review if cost > five hundred bucks.
  • Keeps everything synced: monitoring feeds events, events feed schedules, schedules feed reports.

Visuals: Here's how an ops orchestrator might look—central brain, spawning subs for inventory, shipping, alerts: [simple flowchart: Ops Orchestrator → Monitoring Agent → Event Trigger → Fulfillment Sub-agents → Human Approval → Dashboard]

And this one: daily scheduled report pulling from always-on watchers: [diagram: Cron at 7AM → Data Puller → Aggregator → Email Sender]

Start small—deploy one always-on inventory watcher, add a scheduled report, then let ops spawn a "delay fixer" when things go sideways. Scales fast, cuts fire-drills. Want me to sketch a quick CrewAI setup for your warehouse?

 First, I would like you to explain and research, uh, setups like, for example, OpenCL. Where do you classify them, and for example, team of agents that you can use with Cloud Code or with the Anthropic API. So, how do you classify them or how you, what you can do with them?

Yeah, let's break this down—starting with "OpenCL" I think you mean OpenAI Swarm (easy mix-up, happens all the time). It's not a coding language; it's OpenAI's lightweight multi-agent framework from late twenty twenty-four, still evolving in twenty twenty-six. Think of it as a super-simple way to spin up teams of agents that hand off tasks without heavy orchestration—like bees in a hive, not a strict boss.

Swarm sits in the orchestrated but decentralized camp: agents are autonomous-ish, each with prompts and tools, but they "handoff" control when stuck—like "I'm done researching, pass to writer." No central brain; just routines and shared state. Great for quick experiments, enterprise scaling, or when you want flexibility without LangGraph's complexity.

Here's a clean workflow diagram showing how Swarm routes user tasks through routines and handoffs—see the loops and transfers:

akira.ai
Multi-Agent Orchestration with OpenAI Swarm: A Practical Guide

And this one: a support system example with triage → tech → user flow, all swarming around a hub:

levelup.gitconnected.com
OpenAI's Swarm: The Future of Multi-Agent AI Systems Explained | by Senthil E | Level Up Coding

CrewAI? That's more role-based orchestrated—like a movie crew: define agents as "researcher," "writer," "editor," give them tasks, and it runs sequential/parallel flows. Super easy for ops or content teams. Swarm's lighter, CrewAI's more structured.

Quick side-by-side from twenty twenty-six benchmarks—check this decision tree for picking:

nuvi.dev
Choosing the Right AI Agent Framework: LangGraph vs CrewAI vs OpenAI Swarm | nuvi Blog

And a table comparing features (Swarm wins on speed/flex, CrewAI on ease):

levelup.gitconnected.com
Best AI Agent Frameworks: AutoGen vs CrewAI vs OpenAI Swarm vs LangChain | by Servifyspheresolutions | Level Up Coding

Anthropic's side—with Claude API—you get the Claude Agent SDK (or "Claude Code" for terminal vibes). It's tool-first: Claude models call functions, spawn sub-agents, even use "computer use" for UI stuff. More hierarchical—one main agent orchestrates workers, great for coding, research, or real-world actions. Think: "Claude, build a sales report"—it spawns data puller, analyzer, drafter.

Visual: hierarchical drug-discovery setup with Claude orchestrating subs:

medium.com
5 ways to spawn Multi Agents with the Claude SDK | Orchestra swarms | by Hugo Lu | Mar, 2026 | Medium

And this runtime loop—core agent → tools → subagents:

medium.com
Giving Claude a Terminal: Inside the Claude Agent SDK | by Rick Hightower | Spillwave Solutions

Overall classification:

  • Swarm → Decentralized swarm (light, handoff-based, experimental-to-prod).
  • CrewAI → Team crews (role-driven, easy workflows).
  • Anthropic/Claude → Tool-heavy hierarchical (strong reasoning, sub-agent spawning).

All plug into your ops stuff—like spawning schedulers or monitors. Want to try Swarm for a quick sales pipeline, or Claude for deeper analysis?

49 sources

 Actually, I would like to, for you to review this new idea of OpenCL, CloudBot, also called CloudBot, and also team of agents, uh, from, uh, CloudCode.

Yeah, so "OpenCL" you're saying—turns out it's OpenClaw (big rename from Clawdbot/Moltbot earlier this year, after some trademark drama with Anthropic). It's this viral open-source AI agent thing, super local-first—runs on your hardware like a Mac Mini, acts as a 24/7 private employee. Controls your screen, browser, files, even chats on WhatsApp or Slack. No cloud lock-in, total privacy.

CloudBot? That's basically the cloud version of OpenClaw—same core, but spins up a full Ubuntu desktop in the cloud. You chat in English, it clicks, types, automates—like "schedule LinkedIn posts" or "process invoices." Supports multi-agent teams: launch workers that collab parallel on big stuff. Built on OpenClaw (330k+ GitHub stars now), uses your own Anthropic key—no extra fees. Great for always-on ops without buying hardware.

And "CloudCode"? Sounds like a typo for Claude Code—Anthropic's terminal agent. New hot feature: Agent Teams. You prompt it—"build a sales dashboard with UI, backend, tests"—it spawns a team lead plus teammates. Each has its own window, they chat directly, share a task list, claim jobs like "you do auth, I'll do styling." Parallel magic: frontend guy builds UI while backend handles APIs, tester runs checks—all synced. Way beyond single agents; feels like a dev squad.

Here's OpenClaw's core flow—prompt → preprocess → LLM + tools → output, all local:

pub.towardsai.net
OpenClaw Architecture: Build Production AI Agents (2025) | Towards AI

And CloudBot dashboard—gateway setup, skills list, cron jobs for scheduling:

straiker.ai
How the Clawdbot/Moltbot AI Assistant Becomes a Backdoor for System Takeover | Straiker

Claude Code teams diagram—lead spawns mates, shared tasks, mailbox for direct talk:

cobusgreyling.medium.com
Claude Code Agent Teams. Getting Started with Claude Code Agent… | by Cobus Greyling | Feb, 2026 | Medium

Another one: full team layout with orchestrator, parallel lanes:

blog.laozhang.ai
Claude Code Agent Teams: The Practical Guide to Multi-Agent Development ( 2026) | LaoZhang AI Blog

For your ops crew? OpenClaw/CloudBot shines for routine automation—always-on monitoring, email blasts. Claude Code teams crush complex fixes—like "debug why orders lag," spawn investigators, debate fixes. Mix 'em: use CloudBot for background tasks, Claude for on-demand deep dives. Want a quick setup guide for CloudBot in ops?

 Yeah, I would like you to give me things, uh, step by step and with all the information we discussed about the different form, frameworks, the different type of agents, uh, do a very detailed, uh, report and research on how I should use them for the different workflows in a company to cover the full operations, uh, department first, and also understand how would you deploy them, you know, for a company, because in a company you have employees, they will have their laptops, some of them need to be run in the cloud or in servers, some of, some of the jobs are run locally. Yeah, like think, uh, in very detail about this and give me a solution on how to use the different frameworks and the people also using those agents.

Yeah, here's your full, step-by-step report—pulled from everything we've chatted about, plus fresh twenty twenty-six research. We'll cover agent types, frameworks, ops workflows, and real deployment for a company: laptops, servers, cloud, hybrid. Think of this as your blueprint—start small, measure, scale.

1. Quick Recap: Agent Types & Loops

  • Simple Reflex → React-only, no memory. Use for basic alerts (e.g., "stock low? ping").
  • Model-Based → Keeps state. Good for monitoring logs.
  • Goal-Based → Plans steps. Core for fulfillment: "order → pick → ship".
  • Utility-Based → Optimizes (cheapest route, best stock level).
  • Autonomous → Loops forever (ReAct: reason-act-observe). Always-on like inventory watchers.
  • Loops → Scheduled (cron), event-triggered (new order), dormant (sleep till ping), hybrid (spawn mid-flow).

Visual: Here's how an orchestrator spawns specialists—parallel, no overload:

medium.com
Multi-Agent Systems: Orchestrating AI Agents with A2A Protocol | by Yusuf Baykaloğlu | Medium

And this LangChain-style flow: planner → agents → evaluator:

techaheadcorp.com
AI Orchestration: Designing Multi-Agent Systems For Enterprise-Scale

2. Frameworks for Ops (2026 Top Picks)

  • CrewAI → Role crews: define "Monitor," "Scheduler," "Fulfillment." Easy sequential/parallel. Great for ops teams—human kicks off, agents collab. Dashboard-like. Crew diagram—agents link via tasks:

medium.com
CrewAI: a practical guide for building real agentic workflows | by Ravindra Satyanarayana | Medium

  • LangGraph → Stateful graphs. Custom loops, pauses for approval. Best for complex chains (monitor → trigger → schedule).
  • OpenClaw / CloudBot → Local-first, screen-control. Run on laptops or Ubuntu VPS—always-on, private. Cron jobs for routines (daily reports). CloudBot for 24/7 without hardware. Dashboard with cron setup—schedule wakes, skills:

cybernews.com
OpenClaw Review 2026: How Does It Work?

  • Claude Code Agent Teams → Parallel teams. Lead spawns "Frontend," "Backend," "Tester"—perfect for ops fixes like "debug delay." Hierarchical, shares context. Team layout—parallel lanes, task queue:

blog.laozhang.ai
Claude Code Agent Teams: The Practical Guide to Multi-Agent Development ( 2026) | LaoZhang AI Blog

  • Swarm → Decentralized handoffs. Light for quick experiments.

3. Ops Workflows: Full Coverage

Mix types—always-on for routine, on-demand for fixes.

  • Inventory Monitoring → Always-on autonomous: scans levels, forecasts demand, auto-reorders. Event-triggered on low stock. Use OpenClaw locally (laptop) for privacy, CloudBot cloud for scale.
  • Fulfillment → Goal-based chain: new order → picker agent → packer → shipper. Orchestrator (CrewAI) routes, pauses for human if cost high.
  • Scheduling → Scheduled agents: daily reports at 7 AM, LinkedIn blasts. Cron in CloudBot or LangGraph.
  • Delay/Exceptions → Event-triggered: carrier late? Spawns reroute, notify. Human-in-loop for big changes.
  • Root-Cause Analysis → On-demand: ops says "why slow?"—Claude Teams spawns investigators, debate fixes.

Hybrid orchestrator: central brain mixes all—monitor feeds events, events feed schedules.

4. Deployment: Real Company Setup

Employees' laptops = local/low-latency. Servers/on-prem = secure. Cloud = scale/burst.

Hybrid Model (best practice 2026): Sensitive stuff on-prem/laptop (PII, core logic), heavy compute cloud (inference, bursting). Secure bridge (VPN/API).

Visual: On-prem vs cloud split—control + scale:

medium.com
AI Agent Deployment Strategies: A Practical Guide | by Sahin Ahmed(Data Scientist/MLE) | Medium

Edge/local flow—laptops to cloud hub:

byteiota.com
Edge AI 2026: SLMs and Hybrid Deployment Shift | byteiota

How to Roll Out

  1. Pilot → One team: deploy OpenClaw on manager's laptop for monitoring. Test cron jobs—daily stock email.
  2. Local Laptops → Employees spawn on-demand (Claude Code for analysis). Use Ollama for offline—fast, no API costs.
  3. Cloud/Servers → CloudBot on Ubuntu VPS: always-on fulfillment. Auto-scale with AWS/Azure. Integrate ERP/TMS APIs.
  4. Orchestrator → CrewAI or LangGraph on server—central hub. Employees chat via Slack: "ops dashboard" → spawns subs.
  5. Security/Governance → Audit trails, human pauses on money/legal. Start bounded (narrow rules), expand.
  6. Training → Ops folks learn prompts: "fix delay" → agent chains. Measure: cut fire-drills by thirty percent?

Start with CloudBot for 24/7, add CrewAI for teams. Want a sample config file or pilot script?

49 sources

 or across the different platforms.

Yeah, across platforms it's all about picking the right mix—local for speed/privacy, cloud for scale, hybrid for the win. In twenty twenty-six, ops folks run agents on employee laptops (quick, offline), company servers (secure, always-on), or cloud (bursting, managed). Here's how the frameworks we talked about stack up, with real deployment tips for your ops department. I'll weave in visuals from fresh searches.

First, quick cross-framework map—CrewAI for fast role crews, LangGraph for custom graphs, Claude Agent Teams for deep reasoning, OpenClaw/CloudBot for hands-on automation. No one's "best," but here's the breakdown:

  • CrewAI → Easiest for ops: Define "Inventory Checker," "Delay Fixer"—they collab sequentially. Local on laptops (Python env, Ollama for offline models), hybrid via cloud plugins. Great for prototyping—under twenty lines, low latency. Deploy: pip install, run on dev machines, push to server for production. Employees spawn via Slack bot.
  • LangGraph → Production king: Stateful graphs, pauses for approval, time-travel debug. Model-agnostic—mix Claude, OpenAI, local Ollama. Hybrid sweet spot: local nodes on laptops for low-latency checks, cloud checkpointing (Redis) for persistence. Ops fit: branching flows like "monitor → trigger → schedule → review." Deploy: Docker on servers, Azure Arc for hybrid management—laptops handle edge tasks, cloud bursts inference.

Here's a tier list from devs who've shipped 'em—LangGraph and CrewAI top for ops reliability:

hackernoon.com
The Best AI Agent Frameworks for 2026 (Ranked by Someone Who's Shipped With All of Them) | HackerNoon

And this table nails features—see deployment columns:

levelup.gitconnected.com
Best AI Agent Frameworks: AutoGen vs CrewAI vs OpenAI Swarm vs LangChain | by Servifyspheresolutions | Level Up Coding

  • Claude Agent Teams → Hierarchical power: Lead spawns "Analyst," "Tester"—parallel lanes for fixes like "why shipments lag?" Locked to Claude, so cloud-heavy (Anthropic API), but hybrid via local proxies. Ops: Safety-first (constitutional AI), computer use for browser/ERP. Deploy: API key on servers, employees trigger via dashboard—laptops run light previews.

Team layout visual—shared tasks, direct chat:

  • OpenClaw / CloudBot → Local-first hero: Screen-control, cron jobs—run dormant on laptops (Mac Mini style) or Ubuntu cloud VPS. Privacy king—no cloud lock-in. Ops: Always-on monitoring, event-triggered emails. Deploy: Git clone, local install for employees, CloudBot on AWS for 24/7. Hybrid: Local for PII, cloud for heavy compute.

Dashboard with cron—schedule wakes

33 sources

 Can you continue?

Yeah, picking up—CloudBot's already pulled carrier API logs (dormant till pinged), spots the delay reason: customs hold at port Y. It logs that, hands off to the reroute agent (spawned by the orchestrator on your server—CrewAI style). That one's goal-based: checks alt routes, prices 'em, picks cheapest viable one via utility calc (e.g., "save twenty bucks, same ETA").

It pauses—human-in-loop—sends you a Slack summary: "Reroute via Z? Cost +5%, time -1 day." You thumbs-up; it books, updates tracking, triggers notify agent (scheduled send: email customer "ETA now Friday," auto-draft apology). All synced—monitor feeds back if it works.

This whole chain? Hybrid:

  • CloudBot (VPS) → always-on log pull, low-cost.
  • Server orchestrator → routes, pauses, security.
  • Laptop (ops lead) → quick review, spawn extras if needed (Claude team for deep root-cause if it's recurring).

Visuals to see it: Here's a modular agent flow—traditional process turns into orchestrated groups, perfect for ops handoffs like delays:

linkedin.com
Architecting Agentic Intelligence: A Practitioner's Guide to Translating Business Processes into Modular AI Agent Systems

And this ticket-routing diagram—mirrors our delay: alert → classify → route → notify, with pauses:

linkedin.com
𝗧𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗶𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗿𝗼𝗮𝗱𝗺𝗮𝗽 𝗜 𝘄𝗶𝘀𝗵 𝗜 𝗵𝗮𝗱 𝘄𝗵𝗲𝗻 𝗜 𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗿𝘁𝗲𝗱 𝗯𝘂𝗶𝗹𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗔𝗜 𝗮𝗴𝗲𝗻𝘁𝘀! | Andreas Horn

Hybrid arch example—laptop → server → cloud arrows for logistics:

cloud.google.com
What is end user computing (EUC)? | Google Cloud

Across platforms? CrewAI/LangGraph shine hybrid—Docker on servers, Ollama local for offline, cloud checkpoints. CloudBot nails privacy (local-first), Claude teams add reasoning depth (API burst). Start: pilot on one delay type, track time saved. Need code snippet for that Slack trigger?

10 sources

 I would like to dig a bit deeper. How would you organize all the, all the different agents for an ops department across different platforms?

Yeah, organizing agents across platforms boils down to a hybrid setup—laptops for quick, private, low-latency stuff; on-prem servers for secure, always-on core ops; cloud for bursting scale and heavy inference. In twenty twenty-six, ops teams do this with a central orchestrator (like CrewAI or LangGraph) that routes tasks intelligently: sensitive data stays local, big computes go cloud, everything talks via secure bridges (VPN, Arc).

Here's how I'd layer it for your ops department—inventory, fulfillment, monitoring, delays—keeping employees in control.

1. Local Laptops (Edge/Employee-Level)

  • What runs here: On-demand agents—quick checks, offline analysis. Use Ollama + OpenClaw/CloudBot for privacy (no cloud ping). Employees spawn via desktop or Slack: "analyze this delay log."
  • Why: Zero latency, PII-safe (e.g., client orders), works offline.
  • Framework: OpenClaw for screen/browser control, Claude Code Teams for parallel fixes (lead spawns "investigator" on laptop).
  • Examples: Root-cause bot (pull local logs), supplier scout (draft emails offline).
  • Limit: Light models only—distill if needed.

2. On-Prem Servers (Company-Controlled)

  • What runs here: Always-on monitoring, scheduled routines, orchestrator hub. Dockerized CrewAI/LangGraph for graphs with pauses. Azure Arc-style for hybrid management—enroll servers, central policy.
  • Why: Governance, compliance, low-cost 24/7. Dual ToR switches for resiliency, RDMA for fast storage.
  • Framework: LangGraph for stateful flows (monitor → trigger → approve), CrewAI for role crews ("Watcher," "Scheduler").
  • Examples: Inventory watcher (scans ERP nightly), fulfillment chain (order → pick → ship, pauses on cost).
  • Scale: 3-16 nodes, N+1 HA—Azure Local baseline nails this.

Check this hybrid arch—on-prem core with cloud burst, secure bridge:

medium.com
AI Agent Deployment Strategies: A Practical Guide | by Sahin Ahmed(Data Scientist/MLE) | Medium

And Azure Local network—dual switches, RDMA for ops speed:

learn.microsoft.com
Azure Local Baseline Reference Architecture - Azure Architecture Center | Microsoft Learn

3. Cloud (AWS/Azure/DigitalOcean VPS)

  • What runs here: Heavy inference, bursting, external tools (APIs, web scrape). CloudBot on Ubuntu for dormant agents, Claude API for teams.
  • Why: Auto-scale, pay-per-use—e.g., delay handler spikes during peak.
  • Framework: Swarm for handoffs, LangGraph checkpoints (Redis) for persistence.
  • Examples: Carrier API pull, auto-reroute (elastic compute), batch reports.
  • Bridge: VPN/Arc—sensitive data encrypted outbound.

Central Orchestrator Glue

  • One hub (LangGraph on server) listens: Slack/voice → breaks task → routes (local for PII, cloud for compute).
  • Human loop: Pauses on judgments (e.g., "reroute? thumbs up").
  • Flow: User query → planner → shared pool (memory/tools) → agents → evaluator.

Multi-agent arch—user to planner to subs:

kanerika.com
AI Agent Architecture: Key Components and How It Works in 2026

And LangGraph human-in-loop—break for approval:

zenml.io
LangGraph vs CrewAI: Let's Learn About the Differences - ZenML Blog

Start: Pilot on laptops (OpenClaw monitoring), add server hub (CrewAI), cloud burst for peaks. Track ROI—cut delays thirty percent. Want config steps for Azure Arc + LangGraph?

32 sources

 Yeah, eh, I would like to deep dive again into the, eh, into the structure of how many agents of different types I should be able to to deploy, you know, for a corporate environment, for an operations department, different type of agents, what are the functions, a complete map of all the agents, if you can, if you can define all of them, that would be amazing. Thank you very much.

Yeah, let's map this out properly—your ops department needs a clean, scalable agent lineup, not a mess. In twenty twenty-six, real companies run ten to thirty specialized agents per ops team (think fifty-to-two-hundred employees), with a three-to-one AI-to-human ratio in high-adopters—like one firm hitting three thousand agents for thirteen hundred people. Start conservative: eight to twelve core ones, then spawn extras on-demand.

The key? Hierarchy + specialization—orchestrator at top, sub-agents below, atomic tools at bottom. Match granularity to task: big-picture = orchestrator, daily grind = autonomous, quick fixes = on-demand.

Here's the complete ops map—broken by function, type, trigger, and rough count. All hybrid-deployed (laptop/local for PII, server/cloud for scale).

1. Orchestrator Layer (1-2 agents)

  • Ops Hub — Central brain (LangGraph/CrewAI). Goal-based + utility. Always-on, human-triggered. Routes everything—e.g., "handle delay" → spawns subs.
  • Exception Handler — Utility-based, dormant till alert. Reviews high-risk stuff (cost > five hundred, legal flags).

2. Monitoring & Always-On (3-5 agents)

  • Inventory Watcher — Autonomous, scheduled (nightly scans) + event-triggered (low stock). Goal-based: forecasts, auto-reorders.
  • System Health Monitor — Reflex + model-based. 24/7 logs/servers—pings if CPU spikes.
  • Delay Scanner — Event-triggered (carrier API). Dormant, wakes on ETA miss.
  • Compliance Checker — Scheduled (daily docs). Scans invoices for gaps.

3. Fulfillment & Execution (4-6 agents)

  • Order Processor — Goal-based chain: new order → picker → packer → shipper. Orchestrated, pauses on exceptions.
  • Reroute Optimizer — Utility-based. Event-triggered: finds best alt route.
  • Return Handler — Event-triggered (customer file). Auto-label, refund, inspect.
  • Supplier Scout — On-demand (human spawn). Researches quotes, drafts deals.

4. Scheduling & Routine (2-3 agents)

  • Report Generator — Scheduled (7 AM dashboard). Pulls from monitors, emails summary.
  • Maintenance Scheduler — Scheduled + event. Books repairs, vendor reminders.

5. On-Demand / Human-in-Loop (3-5, spawned as needed)

  • Root-Cause Investigator — Claude Teams style. Human says "why lag?"—spawns analyzer, debater.
  • Crisis Responder — Hybrid: flood? Spawns reroute + insurance drafter. Pauses for approval.
  • Data Analyst — On-demand. Pulls ERP, graphs trends.

Total starter set: twelve agents—one orchestrator, four always-on, four fulfillment, two scheduled, three on-demand. Scale to twenty-five as you add roles (e.g., procurement agent).

Visuals: Here's a multi-agent ops flowchart—user to orchestrator to specialized subs (inventory, fulfillment, delay):

kanerika.com
AI Agent Architecture: Key Components and How It Works in 2026

And hierarchy map—strategic orchestrator down to atomic tools:

linkedin.com
Architecting Agentic Intelligence: A Practitioner's Guide to Translating Business Processes into Modular AI Agent Systems

Deployment tip: Orchestrator on server, always-on on cloud VPS (CloudBot), on-demand on laptops (OpenClaw). Human loop everywhere risky—approval before send/reorder.

This covers full ops: inventory, fulfillment, monitoring, exceptions. Want to tweak for your size—say, ten-person team—or add procurement specifics?

30 sources

 I would like, eh, the design, for example, for a company which is 800 people. It does digital marketing, data analytics, software development, and I would like to understand what is the complete setup, also you have the finance department with 40 people, you have marketing with 10 people, you have HR, uh, with 10 people, and then, uh, you have IT and and support with, uh, 25 people. I would like to understand what would be the setup for a company like that, if you can think through it in detail.

Yeah, for an 800-person company like yours—digital marketing, data analytics, software dev, plus finance (40), marketing (10), HR (10), IT/support (25)—the agent setup scales smart. Total agents: around 40-60 in production, with a central orchestrator handling routing. That's one per 13-20 people, but most are shared across teams—think 3:1 AI-to-human in core ops, lighter elsewhere.

Hybrid deployment:

  • On-prem servers (core hub, secure data)—LangGraph/CrewAI for graphs, always-on.
  • Cloud (bursting, APIs)—CloudBot/Claude teams for heavy lifts.
  • Local laptops (quick, private)—OpenClaw/Ollama for devs/marketers.

Overall Architecture One Enterprise Orchestrator (LangGraph on server) listens via Slack/Teams: "run campaign" → breaks down → spawns subs. Governance: human pauses on money/legal, audit logs everywhere.

Here's the high-level blueprint—core + department layers:

leewayhertz.com
Enterprise AI application: Architecture, development and implementation

And this enterprise agentic flow—perception → cognition → action, perfect for your mix:

astera.com
10 Agentic AI Examples (Use Cases) for Enterprises | Astera

Core/Shared (10-15 agents)

  • Ops Hub Orchestrator — Routes everything.
  • Data Pipeline Agent — Pulls analytics, cleans, feeds dashboards.
  • Security Monitor — Scans for leaks, compliance.
  • Report Scheduler — Daily/weekly cross-dept summaries.

Digital Marketing (15-20 agents, shared with analytics)

  • Content Creator Crew — CrewAI: Researcher → Writer → SEO Optimizer → Publisher. Scheduled posts, A/B tests.
  • Campaign Planner — Goal-based: "launch Q2 ads" → budgets, channels, tracks ROI.
  • SEO Auditor — Event-triggered: crawls site, flags issues, suggests fixes.
  • Audience Analyzer — Utility: segments leads from data.

Visual: Marketing workflow—user input → agents collab:

blog.langchain.com
LangGraph: Multi-Agent Workflows

Data Analytics (10-12 agents)

  • Dashboard Builder — On-demand: "show churn trends" → pulls, charts.
  • Forecast Agent — Autonomous: predicts revenue, alerts deviations.
  • Anomaly Detector — Always-on: flags weird metrics.
  • Query Translator — Natural language to SQL.

Software Development (12-15 agents)

  • Code Reviewer Team — Claude Agent Teams: lead spawns tester, debugger.
  • Bug Hunter — Event-triggered on commits.
  • PR Automator — Drafts, merges low-risk.
  • DevOps Scheduler — Deploys nightly builds.

Finance (8-10 agents)

  • Invoice Processor — Autonomous: validates, pays, flags fraud.
  • Forecast & Risk — Utility: cash flow sims, compliance checks.
  • Expense Auditor — On-demand: scans receipts.
  • Budget Optimizer — Plans spends.

Examples: real finance agents cut invoice time 70-90%.

deviniti.com
Best AI Agent development companies in 2025 | Deviniti

HR (6-8 agents)

  • Onboarding Bot — Goal-based: docs, training, setup.
  • Payroll Runner — Scheduled: processes, compliance.
  • Recruiter Scout — Searches resumes, screens.
  • Performance Tracker — Monitors feedback.

IT/Support (8-10 agents)

  • Ticket Router — Classifies, assigns.
  • System Health — Always-on monitoring.
  • Password Reset — Self-service.
  • Patch Scheduler — Auto-updates.

Start: Pilot marketing + analytics (20 agents), add finance/HR next. Measure: aim 30-50% time saved. Want a phased rollout plan—month one to six?

60 sources

 Can you create a super detailed diagram of all these agents and how I can work in the company? So it's very visual and easy to, easy to understand in very detail so the diagram is understandable by, by everybody.

Yeah, I've got you—let's make this super visual and dead simple. For your 800-person company (digital marketing, analytics, dev, plus small finance/HR/IT teams), here's the big-picture diagram breakdown. I pulled the best clean, hierarchical flows from real enterprise setups—think pyramid with a central brain, branches to departments, and clear arrows so anyone gets it fast. No tech jargon overload.

First, the overall structure—like a company org chart but for AI agents. Central Enterprise Orchestrator (one smart hub) sits at the top. You (or any employee) talk to it via Slack: "run ad campaign" or "fix bug." It plans, delegates, pauses for approval if needed. Below: department "crews" (5-20 agents each), then tools at base. Hybrid layers: laptops for quick stuff, servers for secure core, cloud for heavy work.

Here's the pyramid view—central hub branching out, icons for agent types, deployment at bottom:

agents.sabrina.dev

And this multi-agent pattern—user query flows down to crews, back up with results. Super easy: green arrows = delegation, blue = response:

Department breakdown—color-coded bubbles, counts match your size. Marketing gets most (creative chaos), dev next (code-heavy), finance/HR lighter (routine). Arrows show how orchestrator connects 'em all.

Central Hub (10 agents) → Marketing Crew (15) → Analytics Crew (10) → Dev Crew (12) → Finance (8) → HR (6) → IT/Support (8). Human pauses (thumbs-up icons) on money/legal.

Check this tree-style map—bubbles with triggers like "scheduled," "on-demand," "always-on":

  • Dev bug? "fix login crash" → Dev team (reviewer + debugger) → tests → deploys.
  • Finance invoice? Always-on processor scans, flags fraud → you approve pay.

Picture this:

  • Top bubble: Marketing Orchestrator (green circle) — you chat it "launch summer promo." It plans: "need content? SEO? ads?"
  • Branches out:
    • Content Crew (3 agents) — Researcher (event-triggered: pulls trends), Writer (on-demand: drafts posts), Editor (human pause: you approve tone).
    • SEO Optimizer (1 agent) — Scheduled daily: scans site, flags keywords, auto-suggests fixes.
    • Campaign Planner (2 agents) — Goal-based: budgets channels, A/B tests, tracks ROI—loops back to you for sign-off.
    • Audience Segmenter (2 agents) — Utility: slices data from analytics, targets emails/LinkedIn.
    • Publisher Bot (1 agent) — Scheduled: posts at peak times, auto-schedules.
    • Ad Runner (4 agents) — Always-on: monitors spend, auto-pauses underperformers, re-allocates.
    • Performance Tracker (2 agents) — Event-triggered: new click? Logs metrics, alerts if off.

Arrows: green for delegation (orchestrator → crew), blue for results (back to you), red pause icons on budget/creative decisions.

Finance Mini-Map (yellow bubbles, clean lines):

  • Top: Finance Orchestrator — You say "process March invoices" or "check cash flow." It plans, delegates, pauses on anything over five grand.
  • Branches:
    • Invoice Processor (2 agents) — Autonomous + always-on: scans arrivals, auto-checks data match/fraud/compliance, flags for you.
    • Expense Auditor (1 agent) — On-demand: you upload receipts → it categorizes, spots duplicates, drafts report.
    • Payroll Runner (1 agent) — Scheduled (bi-weekly): pulls hours, calculates, runs compliance, emails payslips.
    • Budget Optimizer (2 agents) — Utility-based: forecasts spend, suggests cuts (e.g., "trim ads 10%"), loops back for approval.
    • Forecast & Risk Agent (2 agents) — Event-triggered: cash dip? Alerts + sims scenarios (worst-case revenue drop).
    • Compliance Checker (1 agent) — Scheduled daily: scans docs for gaps (tax, GDPR), auto-flags.

Arrows: yellow delegation from orchestrator → agents, green results back, red pause dots on approvals/payments.
